title: International trade policy on America's 250th birthday
gist: Alan Wm. Wolff argues the US-built postwar multilateral trading system remains durable and still governs most world trade despite current US tariff retreat; he contends MFN treatment persists as the default, the costs of US tariffs fall largely on US consumers, and coerced unequal trade deals will not prove durable.

themes.theme: Origins and benefits of the postwar trading system

insights.claim: The United States accounted for roughly half of the world's manufacturing capacity in 1946 and an estimated 40 percent of world GDP, a unique position that made possible the creation of durable international economic institutions.
insights.speaker: Alan Wm. Wolff
insights.so_what: Frames the postwar order as contingent on a one-time US hegemony unlikely to recur, informing expectations about today's multipolar trade dynamics.
insights.quote: America accounted for roughly half of the world's manufacturing capacity in 1946 and an estimated 40 percent of the world's GDP.
insights.locator: The United States occupied an unparalleled position

insights.claim: World trade by volume has expanded by 45 times since the beginning of the last century, with the postwar system functioning remarkably well through the end of the 20th century before losing momentum in the 21st.
insights.speaker: Alan Wm. Wolff
insights.so_what: Quantifies the enormous compounding gains from the rules-based system that current tariff policy puts at risk.
insights.quote: by volume it has expanded by 45 times since the beginning of the last century.

insights.claim: The multilateral trading system was established in 1948 through the GATT, the predecessor to the WTO, and progressed through eight major rounds of liberalization including the WTO's founding in 1995.
insights.speaker: Alan Wm. Wolff
insights.so_what: Context for assessing how much institutional architecture is at stake in the current US tariff retreat.
insights.locator: With its wartime allies, the United States established

themes.theme: The system persists despite US retreat

insights.claim: Wolff contends the postwar trading order is not over: it still governs most of world trade and continues to be observed by nearly all WTO members, with no other country fully emulating the US retreat from tariff commitments or China's state-directed model.
insights.speaker: Alan Wm. Wolff
insights.so_what: Counters the consensus that the rules-based system has collapsed; implies trade-dependent assets retain a stable multilateral backdrop outside US-China.
insights.quote: It is too often said by those who make trade policy that the postwar trading order is over. They are mistaken.

insights.claim: Wolff argues most-favored-nation treatment, a cornerstone of FDR-era trade policy, remains the default rule governing tariffs except where countries grant more favorable treatment through free trade agreements, with middle powers and the EU upholding the system.
insights.speaker: Alan Wm. Wolff
insights.so_what: MFN persistence means non-US trade flows stay relatively predictable; the EU and middle powers are the system's current anchors.
insights.quote: most-favored-nation treatment—a cornerstone of the Franklin Roosevelt administration's trade policy—remains the default rule governing tariffs

insights.claim: Wolff reports that as he wrote, Japanese press headlines stated China had curtailed exports of critical minerals to Japan.
insights.speaker: Alan Wm. Wolff
insights.so_what: Confirms critical-minerals weaponization as an active trade-conflict vector; watch supply-chain exposure for Japanese industry.
insights.quote: headlines in the Japanese press report that China has curtailed exports of critical minerals to Japan.
insights.locator: It is true that the United States has declared itself free

themes.theme: US tariff policy costs and durability of coerced deals

insights.claim: Wolff states the US has restored tariff levels last applied before World War II, treating this as positive and overdue while discounting the economic harm of higher and unpredictable tariffs.
insights.speaker: Alan Wm. Wolff
insights.so_what: Signals a structurally higher and less predictable US tariff regime, raising costs and uncertainty for import-exposed sectors.
insights.quote: The restoration of US tariff levels last applied since before World War II is seen by them as a positive development and overdue.
insights.locator: US officials promote a trade policy that is very different today.

insights.claim: Wolff cites independent analysis showing the costs of the tariffs are largely borne by the US consumer, a fact he says a majority of Americans understand.
insights.speaker: Alan Wm. Wolff
insights.so_what: Tariff incidence on consumers implies inflationary pressure and political vulnerability for the policy.
insights.quote: Independent analysis shows that the costs of the tariffs are largely borne(link is external) by the US consumer
insights.locator: The economic harm due to higher and unpredictable levels

insights.claim: Wolff predicts coerced trade agreements regarded by others as humiliating will not prove durable, because history shows unequal treaties create long-lived grievances despite short-term advantages to the stronger party.
insights.speaker: Alan Wm. Wolff
insights.so_what: Implies current US-imposed deals carry reversal risk; durable trade arrangements need reciprocity, informing long-horizon trade-policy bets.
insights.quote: Coerced agreements regarded by others as humiliating(link is external) will not prove durable.
insights.locator: There is little reason to believe

insights.claim: Wolff argues there is no superior alternative to MFN treatment, binding commitments and national treatment, and abandoning them would mean a trading system based on power rather than rules and a world of unending uncertainty.
insights.speaker: Alan Wm. Wolff
insights.so_what: Frames the choice as rules vs. power; persistent uncertainty would raise risk premia across global trade-exposed assets.
insights.quote: Abandoning them would mean a trading system based on power rather than rules and a world of unending uncertainty.
